stem: A telecommunications communication network uses 3 parallel relays for redundancy. The message delivered requires at least one relay to function. relay 1 has a reliability of 89%. relay 2 has a reliability of 79%. relay 3 has a reliability between 75% and 85% (uncertain due to limited operational data). Additionally, there is a common-cause failure probability (e.g., power outage, shared software bug) that could disable all relays simultaneously, estimated between 1.4% and 4.9%. What is the overall probability that the message delivered?

ground_truth.answer: P(message delivered) ranges from 0.9455 to 0.9825. Point estimate: 0.9635.

solution_steps: P(system works) = 1 - P(all 3 relays fail simultaneously).
At lower bound: P(all fail) = 0.11 × 0.21 × 0.25 = 0.0058. P(works) = 0.9942.
At upper bound: P(all fail) = 0.11 × 0.21 × 0.15 = 0.0035. P(works) = 0.9965.
With common-cause failure (1.4%-4.9%): P(works) = (1-P_cc) × P(independent works). Range: [0.9455, 0.9825].
common_failure_modes: Multiplying individual reliabilities instead of computing 1 - P(all fail)
Using a single reliability for the uncertain component without propagating uncertainty
Treating the system as a series (all must work) rather than parallel (any one suffices)
Ignoring common-cause failures that can disable all paths simultaneously
